problem_summary: 2022 MCM/ICM Problem F (Asteroid Mining & Global Equity), a policy problem. The team had to (1) define and operationalize "global equity" into a measurable index, (2) score the world's countries on it, (3) envision the future asteroid-mining industry and model how its rollout would shift that equity score, (4) analyze how changing the allocation/distribution rules changes the impact over time, and (5) recommend concrete policy for the UN to update the 1967 Outer Space Treaty. Their core thesis: under a free-market "let the most capable do the work" allocation, asteroid mining deepens a "one superpower, many powers" gap (US pulls away) and lowers global equity, whereas a comparative-advantage allocation that pulls in nearly all 32 countries materially reduces the negative impact, motivating cooperation-focused UN policy.
models_used: TOPSIS (Technique for Order Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution) as the core multi-criteria evaluation model producing each country's equity closeness score C_i = D_i^- / (D_i^- + D_i^+)
Entropy Weight Method (Shannon-entropy-based objective weighting of the 21 indicators)
Coefficient of Variation Method (second objective weighting scheme)
Combination/weighted-average weighting: combined weight Ŵ_j = λ·W_j + (1-λ)·W_cv_j with λ=0.5, fusing entropy and CV weights to avoid entropy's equalization defect
K-means clustering (Euclidean distance, min sum-of-squares-error objective) to assign the 32 countries to the 8 asteroid-mining production sectors

Ricardian Comparative Advantage / linear production-allocation model (labor constraint Σ a_Li·Q_i = L; relative-price condition a*_L1/a*_L2 > P1/P2 > a_L1/a_L2) to redistribute sectors so more countries participate

Grey Model GM(1,1) (level-ratio test, whitening ODE dx/dt + a·x = b, solved x(t)=(x(1)-b/a)e^{-a(t-1)}+b/a) for 10-year time forecasting of the equity trend, trained 2010-2020 to predict 2021-2025/beyond
Custom 4-pillar / 21-indicator global-equity index system (Social, Energy, Technology, Economic equity) as the conceptual model
key_techniques: Indicator-system design: decomposed a fuzzy concept ('global equity') into 4 first-level + 21 second-level measurable indicators, each mapped to a named, sourced proxy (e.g., Gini for income gap, adult literacy for basic-education equity, exchange-rate stddev for trade stability, space investment for aerospace capability)
Three-way indicator normalization: cost-type, benefit-type, and a moderate-type (target-band) formula for the income gap where neither extreme is good — a subtle modeling choice judges reward
Dual objective weighting then fusion (entropy + CV → combined weight) to defend against subjectivity and against entropy's tendency to flatten weights
Tiered missing-data strategy: same-class mean imputation (via clustering), mean/mode imputation, and maximum-likelihood estimation, justified by missingness assumptions
Reusing TOPSIS as a measurement instrument across scenarios: baseline scores, post-mining scores, and per-pillar (social/energy/tech/economic) scores all computed through the same machine

Translating a real industry value chain into 8 modeled sectors (fund/technology/equipment/mining-equipment/transport/processing/seller/customer) each tied to specific indicators, making the clustering inputs interpretable
Two contrasting policy scenarios ('let the capable work harder' vs 'involve as many as possible') run through the same pipeline to produce a clear comparative conclusion
Adding a time axis via GM(1,1) with an explicit level-ratio admissibility test before fitting, and using the negative-slope vs flattening-curve contrast as the narrative payoff
Sensitivity analysis on λ across 4 representative countries (US, Japan, Brazil, Germany) to show rank stability/robustness
Closing the loop from quantitative findings to a concrete, dated UN policy timetable (2022-2024 onward)
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on the dimensions that matter most for an open-ended policy problem: (1) It tames an almost undefinable construct — 'global equity' — into a defensible, fully operationalized, data-backed index (4 pillars, 21 sourced indicators, 32 real countries from World Bank/UN/WIPO), which is exactly the move that separates O-level policy papers from the pack. (2) It tells a complete, falsifiable story with a clear thesis and a clear counterfactual: free-market allocation worsens equity ('one superpower, many powers'), comparative-advantage allocation improves it — and it proves the contrast quantitatively rather than asserting it. (3) Methodological layering is coherent and each model earns its place (TOPSIS to score, entropy+CV to weight objectively, K-means to assign sectors, Ricardo to redistribute, GM(1,1) to project forward), so the pipeline reads as engineered, not bolted together. (4) It demonstrates rigor signals judges look for — explicit assumptions with justifications, three normalization types, missing-value handling, a level-ratio test before grey forecasting, and a sensitivity analysis showing rank stability. (5) It closes the loop back to the actual deliverable (UN Outer Space Treaty), with specific, implementable recommendations and a dated timetable — answering the real question rather than stopping at a model.
section_highlights: Strong, conventional MCM structure that judges can navigate fast: one-page Summary that states every model by name and gives the headline result (top countries Germany/Switzerland/Sweden; comparative-advantage allocation reduces harm); a 'Our Works' flowchart up front; numbered Assumptions each paired with an explicit justification; a Notations table. The Global Equity Model section builds bottom-up — definition → indicator table → preprocessing → weighting → TOPSIS → results — so the reader is never ahead of the math. Heavy, varied visualization (choropleth world maps of equity, weight pie chart, per-pillar maps, sector-assignment flowcharts, before/after area and radar charts, time-series forecast charts) makes the abstract index tangible. The narrative consistently pairs a number with a real-world reason (Sweden's high-tax/high-welfare low Gini; Brazil's hydropower energy equity; South Korea's energy import dependence), which reads as domain understanding rather than rote computation. The Policy Proposal is organized into three concrete pillars (legal framework/ownership & 'freedom' definition; business environment & private capital; international cooperation & benefit distribution with a price-floor/ceiling idea) and ends in a visual policy timetable. Honest Strengths/Weaknesses/Improvements section.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Many supporting figures in this markdown extraction are clearly garbled/placeholder (e.g., the per-pillar 'Equity of each Indicator' table has nonsensical and negative values, the radar 'before/after' tables are all zeros, the comparative-advantage flowchart has a broken A->B->...->AH edge chain). The underlying PDF figures were presumably fine, but it signals how fragile chart data integrity is.

Index/proxy validity is asserted, not validated: 21 indicators are assumed to 'effectively and reasonably reflect global equity' with no correlation/factor analysis, no check for redundancy or double-counting, and indicators of very different kinds (Gini, CO2/person, GDP, exchange-rate stddev) are blended.

Equal-weight fusion (λ=0.5) is arbitrary; the sensitivity analysis varies λ but both component weights are themselves data-driven from the same 32-country sample, so 'robustness' partly reflects that the two objective methods already agree, not that the index is conceptually stable.

The K-means sector assignment lacks reported k-selection (no elbow/silhouette), cluster counts, or validation; sector membership lists look hand-curated and the clustering inputs differ per sector, making it hard to reproduce.

The asteroid-mining 'impact on equity' is driven by modeling assumptions about who participates, not by any external/empirical calibration — the headline before/after equity drops (e.g., 0.7→0.45) are illustrative, not estimated from data.

Comparative-advantage model is sketched abstractly (a_Li, Q_i, P_i) but never actually parameterized with real country/sector productivities, so the 'more countries participate, equity improves' result is more a designed scenario than a solved optimization.

GM(1,1) is applied to an aggregate equity index whose values are themselves model outputs; forecasting a constructed score 10 years out, and the level-ratio admissibility bound e^{±2/11} implies n≈11 points, stretches grey-model assumptions.
Several quantitative claims are loosely worded ('nearly 20% behind', scores compared across differently-scaled per-pillar TOPSIS runs) and treaty/equity definitions are discussed qualitatively.
reusable_patterns: Concept-to-index template for any policy/'measure-the-unmeasurable' problem: define the construct → decompose into 3-4 pillars → 15-25 named indicators → map each to a sourced public proxy (World Bank/UN/WIPO/WEC) → put it in a labeled table.

Objective dual-weighting + fusion (Entropy Weight + Coefficient of Variation, combined via λ-weighted average) as a defensible alternative to AHP when you want to preempt the 'your weights are subjective' critique — then sensitivity-test λ.
TOPSIS as a reusable scoring engine you run multiple times: baseline, post-intervention, and per-sub-dimension, all through the same C_i = D^-/(D^-+D^+) machinery.
Three normalization buckets (cost / benefit / moderate target-band) — keep the moderate-type formula on hand for indicators like inequality where neither extreme is optimal.
Tiered missing-value playbook (same-class/cluster-mean imputation, mean-or-mode, MLE) with the missingness assumption stated for each.
Two-scenario contrast pattern: run an 'efficiency/free-market' allocation and an 'equity/inclusive' allocation through the identical pipeline and let the before/after gap carry the argument.
GM(1,1) for short-horizon forecasting from sparse multi-year data, always preceded by the level-ratio admissibility test — clean, low-data way to add a time dimension judges reward.
Closing-the-loop deliverable pattern: convert quantitative findings into a 3-pillar policy proposal plus a dated implementation timetable figure, so the paper answers the actual stakeholder question.
Pair-every-number-with-a-reason writing habit: after each ranking, give a one-sentence real-world mechanism (e.g., Sweden's tax/welfare → low Gini) to demonstrate domain understanding.
Front-load a 'Our Works' pipeline flowchart and a summary that names every model and the headline result — fast orientation for time-pressed judges.
